questions.text: Nigeria is located in which part of Africa?
questions.explanation: Nigeria is an area in West Africa bordering the Gulf of Guinea, between Benin and Cameroon.

questions.text: What is Nigeria's total land area?
questions.explanation: Nigeria has a total land area of 934,768 square kilometres.

questions.text: How much of Nigeria's land area is covered by water?
questions.explanation: 13,000 square kilometres of Nigeria's area is covered by water.

questions.text: Nigeria shares borders with which countries?
questions.explanation: Nigeria shares borders with Benin, Cameroon, Chad and Niger.

questions.text: What was Nigeria's population at independence?
questions.explanation: Nigeria's population was 45.14 million at independence.

questions.text: What was Nigeria's population in 2023?
questions.explanation: Nigeria's population increased to over 223 million in 2023.

questions.text: Approximately how many ethnic groups make up Nigeria's population?
questions.explanation: Nigeria's population is made up of about 300 ethnic groups who speak over 500 languages.

questions.text: How many languages are spoken in Nigeria?
questions.explanation: Nigeria's population speaks over 500 languages.

questions.text: Over how many Nigerian ethnic groups are feared to have gone into extinction?
questions.explanation: Over 20 of Nigeria's ethnic groups are feared to have gone into extinction.

questions.text: What percentage of Nigeria's population does the Hausa-Fulani constitute?
questions.explanation: The Hausa-Fulani constitute 29% of Nigeria's population.

questions.text: What percentage of Nigeria's population are the Yoruba?
questions.explanation: The Yoruba constitute 21% of Nigeria's population.

questions.text: What percentage of Nigeria's population are the Igbo?
questions.explanation: The Igbo constitute 18% of Nigeria's population.

questions.text: Which empire was located in the North during Nigeria's pre-colonial history?
questions.explanation: The Borno Empire was one of the independent states in the North of what is now Nigeria.

questions.text: What is the percentage of the Ijaw in Nigeria's population?
questions.explanation: The Ijaw constitute 10% of Nigeria's population.

questions.text: Archaeological records reveal people were living in present-day Nigeria as early as:
questions.explanation: Archaeological records revealed that as early as 9,000 BC or even earlier, people were already living in the area now called Nigeria.

questions.text: Where is the Nok community located in present-day Nigeria?
questions.explanation: Nok is a community in the present day Kaduna State.

questions.text: The Nok Civilisation was particularly known for:
questions.explanation: The Nok Civilisation produced the best early known civilisation in the Nigerian area, especially in agriculture and iron works.

questions.text: How long did the Nok Civilisation last?
questions.explanation: The Nok Civilisation lasted for 7 centuries (500 BCE-200 BCE).

questions.text: The Igbo-Ukwu Civilisation is located in present-day which state?
questions.explanation: Igbo-Ukwu Civilisation covers an area in the present Anambra State.

questions.text: The Igbo-Ukwu Civilisation dates back to which century?
questions.explanation: The Igbo-Ukwu Civilisation dates back to the 9th century AD.

questions.text: Ife Civilisation is located in present-day which state?
questions.explanation: Ife or Ile-Ife is located in the present day Osun State.

questions.text: The Ife Civilisation dates back to approximately:
questions.explanation: Ife Civilisation dates back to around 1200 to 1400 C.E.

questions.text: Which of the following was NOT a notable area of early human settlement in Nigeria?
questions.explanation: Notable areas of human settlements were Iwo-Eleru, Okigwe, Taruga, and Jos-Plateau. Kano is not listed among early settlement areas.

questions.text: The Slave Trade Act of 1807 abolished slavery worldwide, but it continued in Nigeria until:
questions.explanation: Despite the Slave Trade Act of 1807, slave trade continued in Nigeria and Africa generally up to the 1880s and beyond.

questions.text: What agricultural commodities drove European interest in Nigeria during the industrial revolution?
options: Palm oil, palm kernel, cocoa and cotton
questions.explanation: The era of industrial revolution created the need for palm oil, palm kernel, cocoa and cotton.

questions.text: Who amalgamated competing European trading firms into the National African Company?
questions.explanation: Sir Taubman George Goldie amalgamated the competing firms into the National African Company.

questions.text: The National African Company later became which company in 1886?
questions.explanation: The National African Company later became the Royal Niger Company (RNC) in 1886.

questions.text: Lagos was bombarded and annexed by the British between:
questions.explanation: Between 1851 and 1861, Lagos had been bombarded and annexed by the British.

questions.text: British control was extended to Yorubaland between:
questions.explanation: British control was extended to Yorubaland between 1862 and 1893.

questions.text: The Igbo, Ijaw and neighbouring ethnic groups became part of the British colonial empire in:
questions.explanation: The Igbo, Ijaw and other neighbouring ethnic groups became part of the British colonial empire in 1885.

questions.text: The last major northern territory (Borno) to be subdued by the British was around:
questions.explanation: Borno was not subdued until the period around 1914.

questions.text: Who suggested the name 'Niger Area' for Nigeria in 1900?
questions.explanation: Dame Flora Shaw suggested the name Niger Area in 1900. She was a British journalist later known as Lady Lugard.

questions.text: When was Lagos colony merged with the Protectorate of Southern Nigeria?
questions.explanation: Lagos colony was merged with the Protectorate of Southern Nigeria in 1906. This was the first ever amalgamation by the British in Nigeria.

questions.text: When were the protectorates of Northern and Southern Nigeria amalgamated?
questions.explanation: On 1st January 1914, the protectorates of Northern and Southern Nigeria were amalgamated.

questions.text: Who was the Governor of the new Nigeria after the 1914 amalgamation?
questions.explanation: Sir Frederick Lugard was the Governor of the new Nigeria after amalgamation.

questions.text: When did the British government split the Southern provinces into Eastern and Western provinces?
questions.explanation: On April 1, 1939, the British government split the former Southern provinces into Eastern and Western provinces.

questions.text: Caliph Muhammadu Attahiru I and many followers were killed in 1903 at a place called:
questions.explanation: In 1903, Attahiru and many of his followers were killed at a place called Mbormi (about 76 kilometres from the present day Gombe).

questions.text: Which of the following Nigerian rulers did NOT resist British colonialism?
questions.explanation: George Goldie was a British trader. Nigerian rulers who resisted include Jaja of Opobo, Nana of Itshekiri, Ovonranwen of Benin, and Caliph Attahiru I.

questions.text: What was the immediate result of British colonial rule alienating Nigerians?
questions.explanation: The immediate result of British colonial rule alienating Nigerians was the birth of Nigerian nationalism.

questions.text: The Nigerian Youth Movement (NYM) is described as:
options: The first political party in Nigeria
The first true nationalist organization that emerged in Nigeria
questions.explanation: The NYM is best known as the 'first true nationalist organization' that emerged in Nigeria in 1936.

questions.text: In which year was the Nigerian Youth Movement (NYM) formed?
questions.explanation: The NYM emerged in Nigeria in 1936.

questions.text: Who founded the National Council of Nigeria and the Cameroons (NCNC) in 1944?
questions.explanation: The NCNC was founded by Herbert Macaulay and Nnamdi Azikiwe in 1944.

questions.text: The Action Group (AG) was officially launched in which year?
questions.explanation: The Action Group (AG) was officially launched in 1951 by Obafemi Awolowo.

questions.text: The Northern Peoples' Congress (NPC) was formed in 1949 with which leaders?
options: Ahmadu Bello, Abubakar Tafawa Balewa, and Aminu Kano
questions.explanation: The Northern Peoples' Congress was formed in 1949 with Ahmadu Bello, Abubakar Tafawa Balewa and Aminu Kano as its leaders.

questions.text: The NPC developed from which Hausa/Fulani cultural organisation?
questions.explanation: The NPC developed from a Hausa/Fulani cultural organisation called Jamiyyar Mutanen Arewa.

questions.text: Egbe Omo Oduduwa was founded in London in 1945 by:
questions.explanation: Egbe Omo Oduduwa, a Yoruba socio-cultural group, was founded in London in 1945 by Obafemi Awolowo.

questions.text: Nigeria became independent on:
questions.explanation: Nigeria became independent on October 1st 1960.

questions.text: Who was Nigeria's first Prime Minister at independence?
questions.explanation: Nigeria became independent on October 1 1960 with Abubakar Tafawa Balewa as its first Prime Minister.

questions.text: Who became the first President when Nigeria became a Republic on October 1, 1963?
questions.explanation: Nnamdi Azikiwe became the President when Nigeria became a Republic on October 1 1963.

questions.text: The January 1966 military coup was led by:
options: Chukwuemeka Kaduna Nzeogu and Emmanuel Ifeajuna
questions.explanation: The January 1966 military coup was led by Chukwuemeka Kaduna Nzeogu and Emmanuel Ifeajuna.

questions.text: Who was appointed to head the military government after the January 1966 coup?
questions.explanation: Aguiyi Ironsi was appointed to head the military government after the January 1966 coup.

questions.text: The counter coup of July 28, 1966 was led by:
questions.explanation: On July 28 1966, General Murtala Muhammed led a group of military officers dominated by the North to stage a counter coup (July Rematch).

questions.text: Who was appointed to head the government after the July 1966 counter coup?
questions.explanation: Yakubu Gowon was appointed to head the government after the counter coup.

questions.text: Isaac J. A. Boro declared the Niger Delta Republic in:
questions.explanation: Isaac J. A. Boro led a movement that declared the Niger Delta Republic in February, 1966.

questions.text: The Republic of Biafra was declared in May 1967 by:
questions.explanation: Chukwuemeka Odumegwu Ojukwu, the Military Governor of the Eastern Region, declared the Republic of Biafra in May 1967.

questions.text: The Nigerian Civil War ended on:
questions.explanation: The war ended with the surrender of Biafran forces on 15th January, 1970.

questions.text: Approximately how many people died from starvation-related causes during the Civil War?
questions.explanation: The Civil War resulted in starvation-related death of about 2 million people.

questions.text: The Movement for the Actualisation of the Sovereign State of Biafra (MASSOB) was founded in:
questions.explanation: The agitation for Biafra was rekindled in 1999 through MASSOB under the leadership of Ralph Uwazuruike.

questions.text: Boko Haram's common name translates to:
questions.explanation: Boko Haram means 'Western education is forbidden.'

questions.text: The National Youth Service Corps (NYSC) was established in:
questions.explanation: The National Youth Service Corps scheme was established in 1974 to ensure national unity, common national identity and integration.

questions.text: Nigeria has how many official major ethnic groups?
questions.explanation: Nigeria has three official major ethnic groups: the Hausa/Fulani, Yoruba and the Igbo.

questions.text: Which ethnic group is believed to have the least internal diversity?
questions.explanation: It is believed that the Igbo has the least internal diversity.

questions.text: The south-south geopolitical zone of Nigeria comprises mainly:
questions.explanation: The south-south comprises numerous minority ethnic groups.

questions.text: Hausa is the largest ethnic group in northern Nigeria, comprising how many northern states?
questions.explanation: Hausa is the largest ethnic group in northern Nigeria, comprising the present 19 northern states and the Federal Capital Territory.

questions.text: The 1804 jihad that led to the dominance of Hausa-Fulani was launched by:
questions.explanation: The 1804 Usman Dan Fodio jihad led to conquest and assimilation of Hausa states.

questions.text: Before the 1804 jihad, the Hausa states were noted for all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Before the jihad, states were noted for rivalry, corruption, exploitation, slavery, and pagan practices â€” not strong governance.

questions.text: The dominant oral tradition explaining the origin of the Hausa people is:
questions.explanation: The dominant and most acceptable oral tradition that attempts to explain the origin of the Hausa is the Bayajida legend.

questions.text: According to the Bayajida legend, Bayajida fled from Baghdad to:
questions.explanation: Bayajida fled from Baghdad in the Middle East to Kanem Borno.

questions.text: The seven original Hausa states (Hausa Bokwai) include all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: The Hausa Bokwai are Biram, Daura, Katsina, Kano, Rano, Zazzau and Gobir. Kebbi is one of the Banza Bokwai.

questions.text: The seven additional Hausa states (Banza Bokwai) include:
options: Kebbi, Zamfara, Nupe, Gwari, Yauri, Ilorin and Kwararafa
questions.explanation: Kebbi, Zamfara, Nupe, Gwari, Yauri, Ilorin and Kwararafa are referred to as Banza Bokwai.

questions.text: At the apex of the Hausa political system was the:
questions.explanation: At the apex of the political system was the Sarkin kasa, the chief executive who performed political and religious functions.

questions.text: In the Hausa political system, the Galadima was:
questions.explanation: The Galadima was the senior councillor in the Hausa political system.

questions.text: Hausa government was funded through zakat, jangali and tributes. 'Jangali' means:
questions.explanation: Jangali means cattle tax; zakat means alms.

questions.text: Which of the following was an effect of the 1804 Usman Dan Fodio jihad?
options: Adoption of English as lingua franca
Emergence of Hausa aristocracy in place of Fulani aristocracy
Adoption of Hausa as lingua franca and language of administration
Decline of Islam in the region
questions.explanation: The jihad led to the adoption of Hausa as lingua franca and language of administration, among other effects.

questions.text: The Fulani are also known as:
questions.explanation: The Fulani are also known as Fellata, Fula or Fulbe.

questions.text: The Fulani are widely believed to have originated from:
options: The Upper Senegal and Futajalon highlands
questions.explanation: The Fulani are widely believed to have originated from the Upper Senegal and Futajalon highlands.

questions.text: The nomadic Fulani are also known as:
questions.explanation: The nomadic Fulani are also known as Fulanin daji or Fulanin bororo.

questions.text: The sedentary Fulani who settle in towns and villages are known as:
questions.explanation: The sedentary Fulani are also known as Fulanin gida, and they engage in farming, teaching and trading.

questions.text: Among the nomadic Fulani, ownership of cows is:
options: Part of their culture and a definition of their wealth
questions.explanation: Ownership of cows is part of the nomadic Fulani's culture and a definition of their wealth.

questions.text: The Yoruba ethnic group is believed to have been in existence since:
questions.explanation: The Yoruba ethnic group is believed to have been in existence since the period between 2000 to 1000 BC.

questions.text: The traditional homeland of the Yoruba includes:
questions.explanation: Yoruba traditional homeland includes Ile-Ife, Oyo and Iwo-Eleru (near Akure and Owo).

questions.text: According to the Ife version of Yoruba origin, who sent immortals (Oye) from heaven?
questions.explanation: According to the Ife version, Olodumare or Olorun (the Most High God) sent sixteen Oye (immortals) from heaven.

questions.text: In the Ife version of Yoruba origin, who became intoxicated with palm wine?
questions.explanation: Obatala was intoxicated after drinking palm wine, allowing Oduduwa to seize the symbols of authority.

questions.text: According to the Oyo/Samuel Johnson version of Yoruba origin, the Yoruba migrated from:
questions.explanation: According to the Oyo Tradition/Rev. Samuel Johnson version, the Yoruba migrated from Mecca, passed through Yemen and Egypt.

questions.text: The kingdom of Oyo was founded by Oramiyan, a son of Oduduwa, in the:
questions.explanation: The renowned kingdom of Oyo was founded by Oramiyan a son of Oduduwa in the 15th century.

questions.text: At the head of the Yoruba (Oyo) political system was the:
questions.explanation: At the head of the political system was the Alaafin who was regarded as 'the lord of many lands and companion of the gods.'

questions.text: The Oyomesi (council of seven) was headed by the:
questions.explanation: The Oyomesi (council of seven) was headed by the Bashorun (prime minister).

questions.text: According to Yoruba tradition, when an Alaafin is removed, he is expected to:
questions.explanation: When an Alaafin is removed, he is expected to commit suicide.

questions.text: The Ogboni cult in Yoruba politics served as:
options: A counter force and mediator between the Alaafin and Oyomesi
questions.explanation: The Ogboni cult served as a counter force and a mediator in any conflict between the Alaafin and the Oyomesi.

questions.text: The Are-Ona-Kankanfo in the Yoruba political system refers to:
questions.explanation: The Army or Are-Ona-Kankanfo was the military arm of the Yoruba government including 70 war chiefs.

questions.text: The traditional homeland of the Igbo includes which states?
options: Imo, Anambra, Enugu, Ebonyi, Abia and parts of Delta and Rivers
questions.explanation: The traditional homeland of the Igbo are Imo, Anambra, Enugu, Ebonyi, Abia and parts of Delta and Rivers.

questions.text: According to the Nri version of Igbo origin, the ancestor of the Igbo, Eri, descended from:
questions.explanation: According to Nri version, the ancestor of the Igbo, Eri, descended from the sky and sailed down the River Anambra.

questions.text: The Israeli version of Igbo origin is based on:
options: Similarities between Igbo culture and ancient Hebrew culture
questions.explanation: The Israeli version is based on the so-called similarities between the cultures of the Igbo and the ancient Hebrew.

questions.text: The Igbo society has always been known as:
questions.explanation: The Igbo society has always been known as acephalous (segmentary), without a strong centralized ruler.

questions.text: In traditional Igbo society, the centre of government was the:
questions.explanation: In traditional Igbo, the village was the centre of government, described as direct democracy.

questions.text: Amala Oha in traditional Igbo society was:
options: A form of general assembly where male adult members met to perform legislative functions
The title of the Igbo king
questions.explanation: Amala Oha was a form of general assembly in traditional Igbo society where all male adult members met to perform legislative functions.

questions.text: The symbol of authority and justice for each lineage head (Okpara) among the Igbo was the:
questions.explanation: The Okpara's symbol of authority and justice was the Ofo.

questions.text: The Awka or Orlu version of Igbo origin claims they existed since around:
questions.explanation: The Awka or Orlu version claims Igbo societies such as Nri, Awka and Orlu have been in existence since around 2,500 BC.

questions.text: The Benin Empire is also known as:
questions.explanation: The Benin are also known as Bini or Edo and are found in southern Nigeria.

questions.text: The Benin Empire was formed around which century?
questions.explanation: Formed around the 11th century, the Benin Empire was one of the first areas to have contact with the Europeans.

questions.text: The Benin Empire was the first empire on the West African coast to exchange ambassadors with Europeans between:
questions.explanation: The Benin Empire was the first empire on the West African coast to exchange ambassadors with the Europeans between 1481 and 1504.

questions.text: In Bini political organization, succession to the throne was by:
questions.explanation: Succession to the Bini throne was by primogeniture â€” the first surviving son succeeded his father.

questions.text: The Bini society was classified into which two distinct classes?
questions.explanation: The Bini society was classified into two distinct classes: the nobility and the commoner.

questions.text: The second version of Benin's origin states that Oduduwa sent his son ________ to serve as king.
questions.explanation: Oduduwa sent Oranmiyan to serve as king of Benin. His son from an Edo woman later became the first Oba, Eweka.

questions.text: The political system of the Itsekiri is headed by the:
questions.explanation: The political system of the Itsekiri is headed by the Olu who is also the spiritual leader.

questions.text: The Ojoye in Itsekiri political system is:
options: A council of seven nobles and title holders
questions.explanation: The Ojoye is a Council of seven nobles and title holders, similar to the Oyomesi in Yorubaland.

questions.text: The Kanuri are found in which states?
questions.explanation: The Kanuri are found in Borno and Yobe States.

questions.text: The central political system of the Kanuri was headed by the:
questions.explanation: The central political system of the Kanuri was headed by the Mai or king.

questions.text: According to the Saif bin Dhi Yazan version, the foundation of Kanem Borno was laid by an Arab between:
questions.explanation: The foundation of Kanem Borno was laid between the 9th and the 10th centuries CE to an Arab, Saif bin Dhi Yazan.

questions.text: The Nupe are found in which part of Nigeria?
options: Central Nigerian area along the Niger Benue valley
questions.explanation: The Nupe are found in the central Nigerian area along the Niger Benue valley.

questions.text: The head of the Nupe Kingdom is called:
questions.explanation: The Etsu is the head of the Nupe Kingdom.

questions.text: The Igala are found at the confluence of which rivers?
questions.explanation: The Igala are found in central Nigeria at the confluence of Rivers Benue and Niger.

questions.text: The political system of the Igala was headed by the:
questions.explanation: The political system of the Igala was headed by the Attah, assisted by a council of nine chiefs known as Igalamela.

questions.text: The Birom are largely located in which state?
questions.explanation: The Birom are largely located in Plateau State but are also found in Bauchi and Kaduna States.

questions.text: The Birom's rain festival is called:
questions.explanation: Mandyang is the rain festival usually marked between March-April.

questions.text: The paramount ruler of the entire Birom land is known as:
questions.explanation: The stool of the paramount ruler for the entire Birom land is known as Gbong Gwom Jos, established in 1935.

questions.text: The Tiv ethnic group is predominantly domiciled in:
questions.explanation: The Tiv ethnic group, though predominantly domiciled in Benue State, are also found in Nassarawa, Taraba, Plateau and FCT.

questions.text: The Tiv lived within the Cameroun-Obudu complex between 1475 and 1505 before migrating to:
questions.explanation: The Tiv moved down the hills into the Benue Valley through four waves of migration that lasted between 1475-1900.

questions.text: The political system Tor Agbande (Drum chief) was copied by the Tiv from the:
questions.explanation: The Tiv instituted the Tor Agbande political system which they copied from the Jukun.

questions.text: The traditional headquarter of Tivland is in:
questions.explanation: The seat of the Tor Tiv is in Gboko which is regarded as the traditional headquarter of Tivland.

questions.text: The centralized political system of the Jukun is represented by the:
questions.explanation: A centralised political system of the Jukun is represented by the Aku of Wukari.

questions.text: The Jukun Wanu are:
options: Fishermen residing along the river banks of Benue and Niger
questions.explanation: The Jukun Wanu are fishermen residing along the banks of the river Benue and Niger.

questions.text: The word 'culture' is derived from the Latin word 'colere', meaning:
questions.explanation: The word culture is from the Latin word colere, with its original meaning 'to cultivate'.

questions.text: Culture has been broadly defined as:
options: The art and music of a people
The religious practices of a community
The way of life of a people
The economic system of a society
questions.explanation: Culture has been defined as 'the way of life of a people,' including codes of manners, dress, language, religion, rituals, norms and systems of belief.

questions.text: The four elements of culture passed from generation to generation by learning are:
questions.explanation: Culture consists of four elements: values, norms, institutions and artifacts.

questions.text: According to Edward B. Tylor (1871), culture is:
options: The sum total of knowledge, attitudes and habitual behaviour patterns
A complex whole including knowledge, beliefs, arts, morals, laws, customs and habits acquired by man as a member of society
The dynamic aspect of social life
The way of life of a people
questions.explanation: Tylor (1871) defined culture as that 'complex whole which includes knowledge, beliefs, arts, morals, laws, customs and any other capabilities and habits acquired by man as a member of society.'

questions.text: Linton (1940) defined culture as:
options: The way of life of a people
The sum total of knowledge, attitudes and habitual behaviour patterns, shared and transmitted by members of a particular society
A complex whole including knowledge, beliefs and arts
The dynamic aspect of social life
questions.explanation: Linton (1940) viewed culture as 'the sum total of knowledge, attitudes and habitual behaviour patterns, shared and transmitted by the members of a particular society.'

questions.text: Material culture consists of:
options: The words people use and the ideas they hold
Manufactured objects such as tools, furniture, automobiles and buildings
The rules of a game and player's skills
questions.explanation: Material cultures are the aspects of culture that can be seen, felt and touched â€” manufactured objects like tools, furniture, automobiles, buildings, roads, bridges.

questions.text: Non-material culture consists of:
options: The words people use, ideas, customs, beliefs and habits
questions.explanation: Non-material culture consists of the words people use, the ideas, customs, and beliefs they hold, and the habits they follow.

questions.text: A 'symbol' in culture is:
options: A physical object used in worship
A representation of something else that can communicate information in shorthand form
An art form unique to a culture
questions.explanation: A symbol is a representation of something else that can communicate information in shorthand form.

questions.text: The cross symbolizes Christianity while the star and moon symbolizes:
questions.explanation: The cross symbolizes Christianity while the star and moon symbolizes Islam.

questions.text: Norms are of two types:
questions.explanation: Norms are of two types: Folkways (norms performed widely but not necessary) and Mores (strong feelings of right or wrong whose violations attract sanctions).

questions.text: Folkways are best described as:
options: Strong feelings of right or wrong whose violations attract sanctions
Norms performed widely but not necessarily enforced
questions.explanation: Folkways are norms performed widely, but not necessary, e.g. eating with knives and forks.

questions.text: Mores are:
options: Strong feelings of right or wrong whose violations attract sanctions in the society
questions.explanation: Mores are strong feelings of what is right or wrong whose violations attract sanctions in the society, e.g. pick-pocketing, arson, rape.

questions.text: Which characteristic of culture states that it is not inherited biologically but learnt socially?
questions.explanation: Culture is learned through socialization. It is not inherited biologically, but learnt socially by man.

questions.text: The main vehicle through which culture is transmitted from generation to generation is:
questions.explanation: Culture is transmitted not through genes but by means of language. Language is the main vehicle of culture.

questions.text: Which characteristic of culture states that every society has its own unique culture?
options: Culture varies from one society to another
questions.explanation: Culture varies from one society to another. Every society has a culture of its own; cultures are not uniform.

questions.text: Culture is dynamic and adaptive means:
options: Culture is subjected to slow but constant changes over time
Culture is the same in all societies
questions.explanation: Culture is dynamic and adaptive â€” though relatively stable, it is subjected to slow but constant changes over time.

questions.text: Ethnocentrism refers to:
options: The view that different groups have different but equally valid sets of agreements
The view that one's own culture is most superior to others
The tendency to view one's culture as inferior
Difficulty adjusting to a new culture
questions.explanation: Ethnocentrism is the view that one's own group is the centre of everything and one's culture is most superior to others.

questions.text: Xenocentrism refers to:
options: The tendency to view one's culture as inferior and prefer foreign culture
Recognition that different cultures are equally valid
Difficulty adjusting to a new culture
questions.explanation: Xenocentrism refers to the tendency to view one's culture as inferior. An example is the quest for foreign culture among Nigerians.

questions.text: Cultural Relativism refers to:
options: The belief that one's culture is superior
Recognition that different groups have different sets of agreements without the feeling of superiority
The shock of encountering a new culture
questions.explanation: Cultural Relativism refers to the recognition that different groups have different sets of agreements without the feeling that one set is superior to another.

questions.text: Culture Shock is:
options: The pride in one's own culture
A situation where an individual sees something differently because of contradictions and has difficulty adjusting to a new culture
The slow change in cultural elements
The belief that foreign cultures are superior
questions.explanation: Culture Shock is a situation whereby an individual sees something differently from his/her own perspective because of contradictions â€” difficulty to adjust to a new and different culture.

questions.text: Culture lag refers to:
options: The process of passing culture from parents to children
The tendency to view foreign culture as superior
How various elements of culture change at a different pace, creating disruption
The sharing of culture among society members
questions.explanation: Culture lag is a concept that explains how various elements of culture change at a different pace. Technology changes faster than non-material elements, causing disruption.

questions.text: Sub-culture refers to:
options: The dominant culture of a society
Group culture that emerges when people with certain statuses have shared experiences different from others
questions.explanation: Sub-culture refers to group culture that emerges whenever people occupying certain statuses have shared experiences quite different from those of others in the society.

questions.text: Socialization is best defined as:
options: The process of making friends in a community
The process by which persons acquire the knowledge, skills and disposition that makes them integrated members of their society
The process of learning a new language
The exchange of cultural practices between societies
questions.explanation: Socialization is the process by which persons acquire the knowledge, skills and disposition that makes them more or less integrated members of their society.

questions.text: According to Giddens (2005), socialization is the process whereby:
options: The helpless infant gradually becomes a self-aware, knowledgeable person skilled in the ways of the culture
questions.explanation: Giddens (2005) defines socialization as 'the process whereby the helpless infant gradually becomes a self-aware, knowledgeable person, skilled in ways of the culture into which she or he is born.'

questions.text: According to Zanden (1977), in the absence of socialization, society could not:
options: Perpetuate itself beyond a single generation
questions.explanation: Zanden (1977) pointed out that in the absence of socialization, society could not perpetuate itself beyond a single generation.

questions.text: Primary socialization occurs during:
questions.explanation: Primary Socialization occurs in infancy and childhood and is the most intense period of cultural learning.

questions.text: The main agent of socialization during primary socialization is:
questions.explanation: The family is the main agent of socialization during primary socialization (infancy and childhood).

questions.text: Secondary socialization takes place:
options: Later in childhood and into maturity
questions.explanation: Secondary Socialization takes place later in childhood and into maturity, with school, peer groups, and media taking over from the family.

questions.text: The peer group in socialization is defined as:
options: A group defined by the same religion
A group composed of members of roughly equal age sharing equal status
A group of teachers and their students
questions.explanation: The peer group is defined as a group composed of members of roughly equal age sharing equal status as well as pursuing some interests in common.

questions.text: Schools are charged with the formal task of passing on:
options: Many of a culture's symbols, beliefs, values and norms
questions.explanation: Schools are charged with the formal task of passing on many of a culture's symbols, beliefs, values and norms.

questions.text: Which agent of socialization is NOT a formal socializing institution but is a powerful instrument of socialization?
questions.explanation: The mass media is not a formal socializing institution but could be used as a powerful instrument of socialization.

questions.text: Traditional festivals in Nigeria are described as:
options: Periodic celebrations marking significant events and transmitting ideas and philosophy from one generation to another
questions.explanation: Traditional festivals are periodic celebrations marking significant events and transmitting ideas, aspirations and the philosophy of a people from one generation to another.

questions.text: A farming festival is performed to seek for:
options: Adequate and moderate rainfall, adequate food and to pray against crop failure
questions.explanation: A farming festival is performed to seek for adequate and moderate rainfall, adequate food and to pray against general crop failure.

questions.text: The New Yam Festival is an example of which type of festival?
questions.explanation: The New Yam Festival is an example of a farming festival, during which the first harvests are offered to the gods and ancestors.

questions.text: The Osun festival is organized by the Yoruba in honour of Osun, who is the wife of:
questions.explanation: The Osun festival is organized by the Yoruba in honour of Osun, the wife of Sango, the god of thunder.

questions.text: The Osun annual festival takes place in which town?
questions.explanation: The Osun annual festival takes place at the sanctuary of the goddess in Oshogbo in the month of August.

questions.text: The Sharo (flogging) festival is celebrated by the:
questions.explanation: The Sharo or Shadi festival is one of the significant festivals celebrated by the Fulani of northern Nigeria.

questions.text: The Sharo festival is celebrated how many times yearly?
questions.explanation: The Sharo is usually celebrated twice yearly during the dry season and Sallah festival.

questions.text: The Ofala festival is a festival of the:
questions.explanation: The Ofala festival is a festival that accompanies the successful conclusion of a military campaign by the Igbo.

questions.text: The Igue festival is an old festival of which people?
questions.explanation: Igue is an old festival among the Benin people, celebrated in the month of September.

questions.text: During the Igue festival in 1897, which significant historical event occurred?
options: British soldiers invaded Benin, banished their king and looted artefacts
The Oba signed a peace treaty with the British
questions.explanation: It was during the Igue festival in 1897 that British soldiers invaded Benin, banished their king (Oba) to Calabar and killed thousands of people.

questions.text: The Argungu Fishing Festival takes place along which river?
questions.explanation: The Argungu Fishing Festival takes place along the River Rima.

questions.text: The Awon (mass wedding) festival is a popular:
questions.explanation: The Awon (mass wedding) festival is a popular Yoruba festival celebrated to acknowledge the presence of Awon goddess.

questions.text: The Eje Alekwu Festival is the ancestral veneration festival of the:
questions.explanation: The Eje Alekwu Festival is the Idoma ancestral veneration festival celebrated in the months of March and April.

questions.text: Modern festivals in Nigeria include all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Modern festivals are not of Nigerian or African origin and include Christmas, Easter, New Year, May Day, Independence, Maulud, Salah. Osun festival is a traditional festival.

questions.text: The Lebokun festival (Yakun) is celebrated annually in:
questions.explanation: The Lebokun festival (Yakun) is a sacred festival celebrated annually in Upep, northeast of Calabar.

questions.text: Social vices are best defined as:
options: Deviant behaviours such as illicit sex, drug addiction, evil or immoral behaviour
questions.explanation: Social vices are deviant behaviours such as illicit sex, drug addiction, evil or immoral behaviour such as murder, examination malpractice, thuggery and such other criminal tendencies.

questions.text: Social vices are at variance with social norms and values which include all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Social vices are at variance with social norms and values such as beliefs, honesty, hard work, customs and traditions. Cultism and thuggery are themselves social vices.

questions.text: The National Orientation Agency (NOA) was established on:
questions.explanation: On 23rd August, 1993, an Act was passed to establish the National Orientation Agency.

questions.text: MAMSER stands for:
options: Mass Mobilization for Self Reliance, Social Justice, and Economic Recovery
Movement for African Mass Social Economic Reform
Military Action for Social and Economic Revolution
Mass Association for Social Mobilization and Economic Reform
questions.explanation: MAMSER stands for Mass Mobilization for Self Reliance, Social Justice, and Economic Recovery.

questions.text: MAMSER was inaugurated on:
questions.explanation: MAMSER was inaugurated on July 25th, 1987.

questions.text: MAMSER was undertaken under which Nigerian president?
questions.explanation: MAMSER was an exercise in political orientation in Nigeria undertaken by former president Ibrahim Babangida.

questions.text: The War Against Indiscipline (WAI) was a programme of which regime?
questions.explanation: The War Against Indiscipline was a programme of the Buhari regime.

questions.text: The Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) was established under:
questions.explanation: The EFCC (and ICPC) were established under the Obasanjo regime.

questions.text: The West African Pilot and The Negro World were prominent newspapers that contributed to:
questions.explanation: The West African Pilot and The Negro World were the most prominent newspapers that contributed seriously to the emergence of Nigerian nationalism.

questions.text: The Federal Character system ensures:
options: All positions go to the majority ethnic group
Equitable and balanced appointment into public offices (enshrined in Section 14 of the 1999 Constitution)
questions.explanation: Federal Character system ensures equitable and balanced appointment into public offices as enshrined in Section 14 of the 1999 Constitution of Nigeria.

questions.text: The Nok Civilisation covered areas in central Nigeria including:
questions.explanation: Nok civilisation covers many areas in central Nigeria, including Jos Plateau and Abuja.

questions.text: The resistance movement at Mbormi in 1903 was approximately how many kilometres from present-day Gombe?
questions.explanation: The resistance movement was at a place called Mbormi (about 76 kilometres from the present day Gombe).

questions.text: The Yoruba are also found outside Nigeria in which countries?
options: Eastern Benin (Dahomey), Ghana, Togo and Brazil
questions.explanation: Yoruba are also found in eastern Benin (formerly Dahomey), northern Ghana, Togo and Brazil.

questions.text: The Yoruba economy was built primarily on:
questions.explanation: The economy of the Yoruba was built primarily on agriculture. Other important aspects were trading, blacksmithing, carving and sculpturing.

questions.text: The basic components of the traditional Igbo economy were:
questions.explanation: The basic components of the traditional economic system of the Igbo were agriculture, trade and industry.

questions.text: In traditional Igbo agriculture, which crop was well known?
questions.explanation: In Igbo agriculture, cultivation of root crops such as yam was well known.

questions.text: The Hausa economy was NOT built on which of the following?
questions.explanation: The Hausa economy was built on agriculture, industry (leather works, weaving, dyeing) and trade (especially long distance trade). Deep sea fishing is not part of it.

questions.text: The 1962 and 1963 census controversies in Nigeria were rooted in:
options: Allegations of manipulation of figures by different regions for political dominance
Lack of funds to conduct census
questions.explanation: The census was controversial because of allegations of manipulation of figures by different regions, done in consideration of dominance and control of government.

questions.text: Values in culture are:
options: Physical objects produced by a culture
Ideas about what seems important in life
The structures of society where norms are taught
questions.explanation: Values include ideas about what seems important in life.

questions.text: Institutions in the context of culture are:
options: The structures of society within which values and norms are taught and learned
questions.explanation: Institutions are the structures of a society within which values and norms are taught and learned.

questions.text: The Kanuri political system's state council and advisory body was called the:
questions.explanation: The Nokena (Council of the twelve) served as the state council and advisory body in the Kanuri political system.

questions.text: According to the third version of Nupe origin, the founder Edegi arrived from Idah to found the kingdom in the:
questions.explanation: According to the third version, Edegi (the founder) arrived from Idah to found the Nupe kingdom in the 16th century.

questions.text: One of the functions of the NOA is to:
options: Enlighten the general public on Federal Government policies, programmes and activities
questions.explanation: One function of the NOA is to enlighten the general public on Federal Government policies, programmes and activities.

questions.text: Between 1957 and 1959, independence was granted to regional governments in:
questions.explanation: Between 1957 and 1959, independence was granted to Regional governments in the East, West and North.

questions.text: The Idu Festival is a feast of the creator observed by the:
questions.explanation: The Idu Festival (Nembe) is a feast of the creator who the Nembe people collectively respect.

questions.text: The Argungu Fishing Festival dates back to the:
questions.explanation: The Argungu Fishing Festival dates back to the 18th century and has its origin in the traditional fishing festival called Su.

questions.text: Isaac Boro's major grievance in declaring the Niger Delta Republic was:
options: Inadequate share of the proceeds of oil wealth derived from their land
The absence of schools in the Niger Delta
questions.explanation: Their major grievance was that they did not enjoy adequate share of the proceeds of the oil wealth that was derived from their land.

questions.text: Which of the following is an ethnic group in Northern Nigeria?
questions.explanation: The Kanuri is one of the ethnic groups in Northern Nigeria. Ijaw, Ibibio, and Ikwere are Southern Nigerian groups.

questions.text: The area had been exposed to Islamic religion since the:
questions.explanation: Apart from influence of the jihadists, the Hausa area had been exposed to Islamic religion since the 14th and 15th centuries.

questions.text: A Portuguese explorer named Ruy de Sequeira arrived in Benin during the reign of:
questions.explanation: A Portuguese, Ruy de Sequeira, arrived during Oba Ewuare's reign in 1472.

questions.text: During the Igue festival, wearing of red cap is prohibited except for members of:
questions.explanation: During the Igue festival wearing of red cap is prohibited except for members of the armed forces.

questions.text: The 'July Rematch' of 1966 included officers such as all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: The July Rematch officers included T.Y. Danjuma, Ibrahim Babangida, Shitu Alao, Muhammadu Buhari, Sani Abacha, B.S. Dimka, and others. Ojukwu was the Eastern Region Military Governor, not part of the counter coup.

questions.text: Religious institutions (churches and mosques) as agents of socialization are interested in:
options: The moral aspects of personality development of both children and adults
questions.explanation: Religious institutions are interested in the moral aspects of personality development of not only the children but the adults too.

questions.text: According to Yoruba tradition, where did Oduduwa set down the pieces of iron and lump of earth to create the world?
questions.explanation: They descended at Oke Oramfe in Ile-Ife where Oduduwa set down the five pieces of iron and placed the lump of earth on them.

questions.text: The Benin version of Igbo origin says which groups claim Benin origin?
options: The Onitsha and the Igbo on the other side of River Niger (Delta Igbo)
questions.explanation: The Onitsha and the Igbo on the other side of the River Niger (Delta Igbo) claim Benin origin.

questions.text: The Igbo-Ukwu Civilisation proved existence of early civilisation in which areas?
options: Government, religion, agriculture, hunting and trade
questions.explanation: Igbo-Ukwu dates back to the 9th century AD and proved existence of early civilisation in government, religion, agriculture, hunting and trade.

questions.text: An example of ethnocentrism is:
options: Nigerians preferring foreign goods and culture
Europeans' 'civilising mission' during colonialism and the Nazi holocaust
The recognition that all cultures are equally valid
Difficulty adjusting to a new culture abroad
questions.explanation: Examples of ethnocentrism include the 'civilising mission' of Europeans during colonialism and the Nazi holocaust (determination to exterminate Jews as an inferior race).

questions.text: The Kanuri constitute what percentage of Nigeria's population?
questions.explanation: Kanuri constitute 4% of Nigeria's population.

questions.text: The Tiv constitute what percentage of Nigeria's population?
questions.explanation: The Tiv constitute 2.5% of Nigeria's population.

questions.text: The Ibibio constitute what percentage of Nigeria's population?
questions.explanation: The Ibibio constitute 3.5% of Nigeria's population.

questions.text: The Birom language belongs to which family of languages?
questions.explanation: The Birom language belongs to the large Niger-Congo family of languages.

questions.text: NKST (Nongu u Kristu u i ser u sha Tar) stands for the Universal Reformed Christian Church and is significant because:
options: It was founded by the British missionaries
It is one of the largest indigenous Christian Missions in Nigeria, proof of Christianity's impact on Tivland
It was established by the colonial government
It was founded by Usman Dan Fodio
questions.explanation: NKST â€” Nongu u Kristu u i ser u sha Tar (Universal Reformed Christian Church) â€” is one of the largest indigenous Christian Missions in Nigeria and is a proof of the tremendous impact of Christianity in Tivland.
